questions.prompt: Acquirer values target standalone at $1B and projects $200M of synergies. What is the maximum the acquirer should pay (max offer) without destroying acquirer-shareholder value? Answer in $B.
questions.explanation: P_max = V_T + S = 1.0 + 0.2 = $1.2B. Paying more than this transfers more than the synergy to target holders.

questions.prompt: Stock deals (vs cash deals) are MORE common when:
choices: The acquirer believes its stock is undervalued
The acquirer believes its stock is overvalued ("paying with expensive paper")
questions.explanation: Stock deals dilute existing acquirer shareholders; using overvalued stock is cheaper. Markets often react negatively to stock-deal announcements because they infer overvaluation.

questions.prompt: Common sources of post-merger value destruction include:
choices: Overpaying due to winner's curse in competitive bidding
Acquirer's stock price always falls regardless of the deal
questions.explanation: Overpayment, integration risk, and synergy fantasies are the three big destroyers. Stock-price reactions vary by deal quality — they're not uniformly negative.

questions.prompt: Empirically, what fraction of acquisitions destroy value for the ACQUIRER (per 3-5 year post-merger studies)?
questions.explanation: The empirical record is sobering: 40-60% of acquisitions reduce acquirer value. Targets generally do well (control premium); acquirers often don't. Disciplined serial acquirers (Berkshire) are the exception.

furtherReading.title: Observations on the Adobe-Figma acquisition termination
furtherReading.why: Adobe-Figma's collapsed $20B deal with a $1B breakup fee is one of the most discussed value-destroying / value-preserving M&A episodes of the cycle.
